title: Startup de Florida lanza la primera demostración comercial de tecnología de batería nuclear en órbita espacial
summary: La startup City Labs ha lanzado el cubesat BOHR para probar su sistema de potencia betavoltaico NanoTritium, alimentado por tritio, que genera electricidad sin necesidad de luz solar, con el objetivo de habilitar misiones de larga duración en espacio profundo y regiones oscuras de la Luna.
imageAlt: Startup de Florida lanza la primera demostración comercial de tecnología de batería nuclear en órbita espacial
aiSummary: La startup City Labs ha lanzado el cubesat BOHR para probar su sistema de potencia betavoltaico NanoTritium, alimentado por tritio, que genera electricidad sin necesidad de luz solar, con el objetivo de habilitar misiones de larga duración en espacio profundo y regiones oscuras de la Luna.
keyPoints: La startup City Labs lanzó el 7 de julio el cubesat BOHR en la misión Transporter-17 de SpaceX para probar su sistema de potencia betavoltaico NanoTritium.
El sistema utiliza tritio, un isótopo radiactivo del hidrógeno, para generar electricidad de forma autónoma sin necesidad de luz solar, con una potencia de salida en microwatios.

La demostración, financiada parcialmente por contratos de la NASA y el Pentágono, busca validar la tecnología para misiones de larga duración en espacio profundo y regiones permanentemente sombreadas de la Luna.

La compañía planea lanzar en 2027 una unidad calefactora de radioisótopos con tritio para evitar que los componentes de naves espaciales se congelen durante periodos sin luz solar, como la noche lunar de dos semanas.
whyMatters: Esta tecnología supone un hito para la autonomía de misiones espaciales que no dependen de paneles solares, abriendo la puerta a operaciones en entornos donde la energía solar es insuficiente o inexistente, como el espacio profundo o los cráteres permanentemente sombreados de la Luna, puntos de interés clave para la exploración lunar y la posible extracción de recursos. La aprobación regulatoria de la FAA para el lanzamiento de materiales radiactivos con fines comerciales sienta un precedente clave para el desarrollo de la industria espacial privada en el sector de la energía nuclear espacial, un campo dominado históricamente por agencias gubernamentales. Para el ecosistema espacial europeo y español, esta tecnología representa una oportunidad de colaboración para misiones de larga duración, incluyendo posibles aplicaciones en satélites de observación terrestre o misiones de exploración lunar en las que participen entidades de la UE y la Agencia Espacial Europea, reduciendo la dependencia de sistemas de energía convencionales.

multimedia.alt: Imagen del cubesat BOHR de City Labs terminado antes de su lanzamiento a órbita
multimedia.caption: El cubesat BOHR, con el sistema de potencia betavoltaico NanoTritium integrado, fue lanzado el 7 de julio de 2026 en la misión Transporter-17 de SpaceX. Crédito: City Labs / SpaceNews

fullArticle:
Una startup con sede en Florida ha dado un paso histórico en la exploración espacial comercial al lanzar el pasado 7 de julio el cubesat BOHR, la primera demostración comercial de una tecnología de batería nuclear diseñada para satélites. La misión, transportada en la rideshare Transporter-17 de SpaceX, tiene como objetivo validar el sistema de potencia NanoTritium de la empresa City Labs, un generador betavoltaico alimentado por tritio que puede producir electricidad de forma autónoma sin necesidad de luz solar. La demostración, financiada parcialmente por contratos de la NASA y el Pentágono, busca ampliar el rango de misiones espaciales que pueden operar durante años en entornos donde los paneles solares no son viables, como el espacio profundo o las regiones permanentemente sombreadas de la Luna.

[MULTIMEDIA: https://i0.wp.com/spacenews.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/BOHR_Assembly_Finished.png.webp?fit=539%2C660&ssl=1]

El sistema NanoTritium funciona convirtiendo directamente la energía liberada por la desintegración radiactiva del tritio en pequeñas cantidades de electricidad, con una potencia de salida medida en microwatios, insuficiente para alimentar un satélite completo pero ideal para electrónica de baja potencia que debe funcionar de forma continua durante años sin interrupciones. Cabe destacar que el cubesat BOHR no depende exclusivamente de esta tecnología nuclear: su bus satelital funciona con paneles solares convencionales, mientras que el sistema NanoTritium alimenta únicamente la carga útil evaluada en la misión, con el objetivo de validar su rendimiento en órbita sin reemplazar los sistemas eléctricos principales de la nave. La misión también supone la primera misión nuclear comercial que utiliza el proceso de aprobación de lanzamientos de la Administración Federal de Aviación (FAA) establecido bajo el Memorando Presidencial de Seguridad Nacional 20, un marco regulatorio creado para regular el lanzamiento de naves que transportan materiales radiactivos. City Labs asegura que sus sistemas de potencia basados en tritio operan con niveles de radiación extremadamente bajos y están diseñados para un manejo, transporte e integración seguros con vehículos de lanzamiento comerciales, contando además con el apoyo de contratos de investigación del Pentágono, la Fuerza Aérea de Estados Unidos, NASA y SpaceWERX.

La compañía planea lanzar en 2027 una demostración en órbita de una Unidad Calefactora de Radioisótopos (RHU) también alimentada por tritio. A diferencia del sistema de BOHR, que genera electricidad, la RHU produce calor para evitar que componentes, baterías e instrumentos científicos se congelen durante periodos prolongados sin luz solar, como la noche lunar de dos semanas en los polos o el interior de cráteres permanentemente sombreados. Esta tecnología supone una alternativa comercial a las RHU de plutonio utilizadas históricamente por la NASA en misiones de exploración planetaria, y abre la puerta a colaboraciones con entidades espaciales europeas y españolas para misiones de exploración lunar y satélites de observación de larga duración, reduciendo costes y dependencia de suministros gubernamentales.

title: I made my agent more capable and it got worse
summary:
Builder Journal · ARC Prize 2026

There is a moment in every role-playing game where you load your character with so much heavy gear that they can barely walk. Strongest sword in the game, can't reach the fight. I did the machine-learning version of that this month. I kept making my agent more capable, and the scoreboard kept punishing me for it, and it took me two tries to understand that the upgrades were the problem.

A quick frame, in case this is your first entry in this thread: I'm in the ARC Prize 2026, building an agent that has to learn small games it has never seen, with no instructions. As the benchmark's creator measured it, the hardest part by far is the piece that figures out the rules of a game by experimenting on it. So that piece is where I have been pouring my effort.

The obvious upgrade

The obvious way to make that piece better is to teach it more kinds of games. If it can model three families of puzzle today, teach it a fourth, and it should win more. So I did exactly that. I built support for a new class of game it could recognize and solve, wrote it carefully, tested it, and confirmed the thing I wanted to confirm: the agent now beat a game it provably could not beat the day before. Real, verified, new capability. Not a story I was telling myself, a genuine new skill on the board.

Then I submitted, and the score went down.

Twice

This is the part I want to be honest about, because one bad result is noise and two is a pattern. My agent's attempts to use this theory-building component had already been underwhelming on the real board, landing around 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 across earlier tries, all of them under the 0.25 my plain, careful agent scores when it does not reach for the fancy component at all. The fourth skill was supposed to turn that corner. Instead the next submission came in at 0.04, the worst of the lot. I had added ability and the number had dropped, again.

So I stopped adding and started counting. I ran a survey across twenty-five of the practice games to see what my agent was actually doing on each one. The shape of it was sobering. It cleanly won five. It correctly recognized five more as hopeless and skipped them. And on most of the rest, it poked and prodded at the game, spent real effort trying to build a theory, and then gave up with nothing to show for it.

The reason, when I finally cornered it

The failure was not in the new skill. It was in the cost of reaching for the skill at all. Every time the agent tries to build a theory of a game, it has to spend real moves poking at that game to test its guesses. On a game it can crack, that effort is an investment that pays off in a win. On a game it cannot crack, the effort is pure loss, spent and gone, and most of the hidden games turn out to be ones it cannot crack.

So every new skill I added had the same hidden effect. It made the agent attempt more games, which meant it spent more effort on more games it would ultimately lose anyway, and paid that toll on every single one. I thought I was buying wins. I was buying more expensive losses. Adding the fourth skill is exactly what dragged 0.09 down to 0.04: more reasons to try, more failed attempts, more cost.

What it means

This is the trap of optimizing the wrong variable. I had been quietly counting how many games my agent could solve, treating raw capability as the goal. The competition does not pay for capability. It pays for net result, and a capability that costs more than it returns is a liability wearing the costume of progress.

The lever I thought I was pulling, make it able to do more, was the wrong lever. The right one is closer to the opposite, and that part I am keeping to myself for now, because it is still live and still my edge. What I can hand over freely is the principle underneath it: more powerful is not the same as better. A bigger toolbox is only an asset if you also know when to keep it shut. I spent weeks adding tools, and the breakthrough, when it comes, is going to be a lesson in restraint, not capability.

Where I'm standing right now

0.25, same as ever, and the plain agent still wears the crown over every cleverer version I have built. The difference is that I now understand why all that added capability never moved the number, which is not nothing. Knowing precisely why a thing fails is the toll you pay on the road to the thing that works.

Next entry: the time I spent two full work sessions reasoning from something I believed about my own code that turned out to be flatly untrue.

More in this series

  • ← Start here: I'm trying to teach a machine to play a game it has never seen, what ARC-AGI-3 is, and the bet I'm making.
  • Why the leaderboard is the only oracle: I looked unbeatable in practice and lost the real fight, why my home bench doesn't get a vote anymore.
  • The full ARC Prize 2026 thread, every entry from this competition.
  • The Builder Journal, the live log across all my competitions.

This is part of an ongoing builder's log written from inside live competitions. You're reading where I was, not where I am.

fullArticle:
Xreal's first pair of budget smart display glasses impresses in every way. With ground-breaking modular construction, 3D-printing template files, multiple official shades and styles to choose from, and the lightest frames on the market, these are incredibly comfortable to wear.
The SeeYa micro-OLED panels are not only the smart glasses industry's first native 10-bit displays but also feature impressive 3840Hz PWM dimming for incredible eye comfort. These are also the first pair of affordable wearable displays with spatial tracking, further adding value to the package. The speakers could be louder, but they do sound great and are one of the only middling aspects of an otherwise truly stellar pair of glasses.

title: Your Postgres Is Quietly Rotting — Here Are the Queries That Show It
summary:
It's Friday evening. An endpoint that normally answers in 200 milliseconds is suddenly taking eight seconds. You open Grafana. Every graph is green. CPU is calm, memory is fine, the disk isn't full. By every dashboard you have, the database is healthy.

It is not healthy.

This is the failure mode monitoring is worst at: the server is unmistakably alive, so nothing alerts, while inside the database something is slowly rotting. A table has bloated. An index nobody uses is dragging down every INSERT. A forgotten transaction is sitting open, holding a lock and quietly making everything worse. None of it crashes. It just degrades, a little at a time, until one Friday evening it tips over.

The good news is that Postgres will tell you all of this — you just have to ask. The queries below run on bare PostgreSQL (13 or newer; one version note along the way), need no agent and no paid monitoring, and use an extension in exactly one place where it genuinely earns it. Open psql and check your own database as you read.

1. The cheapest signal: dead rows

Start here, because it costs nothing and catches the most. Postgres never deletes a row in place. An UPDATE or DELETE leaves behind a dead tuple — an old version of the row — and autovacuum cleans those up later. Until it does (or if it can't keep up), the dead rows sit in the table, taking space and forcing every scan to page past them.

The fastest look is pg_stat_user_tables, always available, no extension:

SELECT
    schemaname,
    relname AS table,
    n_live_tup,
    n_dead_tup,
    round(n_dead_tup * 100.0 / nullif(n_live_tup + n_dead_tup, 0), 1) AS dead_ratio,
    last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE n_dead_tup > 0
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC
LIMIT 20;

A dead_ratio above ~20% on a large table is worth investigating. And watch for a table where the ratio is high and last_autovacuum is empty — that means autovacuum has never successfully run on it, which is its own red flag (we'll see why in section 5; the whole story converges there).

What to do. If autovacuum is simply behind, lower its threshold for that specific hot table — autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor defaults to 0.2, and dropping it to 0.05 for a busy table makes vacuum trigger far sooner. Bloat that has already accumulated, though, a plain VACUUM won't fix — it marks the space reusable but doesn't hand it back to the OS. VACUUM FULL does, but it takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and rewrites the whole table, so the table is unavailable for the entire operation — not something to run on a live system. The production-safe option is pg_repack, which does almost the same compaction without the long lock, at the cost of disk space for a copy and a requirement that the table have a primary key.

2. What's actually eating the server's time

This is the one query worth enabling an extension for: pg_stat_statements. You very likely already have it — it's on by default in many builds and almost all managed Postgres. If not, add pg_stat_statements to shared_preload_libraries in postgresql.conf, restart, and CREATE EXTENSION pg_stat_statements;. It is the single most useful diagnostic tool Postgres ships.

It accumulates, per unique query, how many times it ran and how much time it consumed:

SELECT
    calls,
    round(total_exec_time::numeric, 1) AS total_ms,
    round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2)  AS mean_ms,
    round((100 * total_exec_time / sum(total_exec_time) OVER ())::numeric, 1) AS pct,
    query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 15;

The crucial, slightly counterintuitive part is sorting by total_exec_time, not the average. A query that runs in 4 ms looks harmless — but if it's called two million times a day, it costs your server far more than a three-second report run twice daily. Total time catches that; the mean hides it. The pct column shows what fraction of all database time each query eats, and usually the top three or four rows are your entire performance backlog.

A version note: in PostgreSQL 12 and earlier these columns were total_time and mean_time; 13 split planning from execution, which is where total_exec_time / total_plan_time come from. Queries here are normalized — literal values replaced with $1, $2 — so the same query shape with different parameters collapses into one row, which is exactly what you want. When you've fixed something and want a clean measurement, SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset(); zeroes the stats. Then take the offending query to EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to see where it's actually stalling.

3. Big tables that keep getting read end to end

A sequential scan isn't evil. On a small table the planner deliberately picks one, because reading the whole thing is faster than bouncing through an index. The problem is a large table being read in full, often.

SELECT
    schemaname,
    relname AS table,
    seq_scan,
    idx_scan,
    seq_tup_read,
    CASE WHEN seq_scan > 0 THEN seq_tup_read / seq_scan ELSE 0 END AS avg_rows_per_scan,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE seq_scan > 0
ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC
LIMIT 20;

seq_tup_read is the total number of rows read by sequential scans. When that number is huge for a big table and idx_scan next to it is small, that table is being walked in full on the regular. avg_rows_per_scan tells you how heavy each individual scan is.

One caveat: this gives you candidates, not a diagnosis. It won't tell you which column to index — only where to look. Pair it with the query from section 2: "here's the statement eating time" plus "here's the table being scanned end to end" usually meet at a single point, and that's where the missing index is. And don't reflexively index a thousand-row table — for that one, the sequential scan is correct. Leave it alone.

Reading the plan EXPLAIN hands you

Sections 2 and 3 both end the same way: you've found a query worth fixing, so you run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on it — and then you're staring at a tree of nodes, which is its own skill. A few things to look for catch most problems without a deep dive:

  • Actual vs estimated rows. Each node shows an estimated rows=... and, with ANALYZE, an actual rows=.... When those differ by orders of magnitude, the planner is working from bad statistics and probably chose a bad plan — often fixed by running ANALYZE on the table or raising its statistics target.
  • The expensive node. Read inner to outer and find where the actual time piles up. That node doing the most work is your target, not the whole tree.
  • Buffers. With BUFFERS on, each node reports shared hit (served from cache) versus shared read (fetched from disk). A node reading thousands of blocks from disk is exactly the I/O you want to remove, usually with an index or by fetching fewer rows.
  • Rows Removed by Filter. A scan that reads a million rows and filters down to ten is begging for an index on the filtered column.

You don't have to parse two hundred lines of plan by hand. Find the node with the surprising row count or the heavy disk reads, fix that one thing, and re-measure.

4. Indexes that only get in the way

Now the opposite problem. A surplus index isn't just "taking up space." It slows down every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE on its table — each write has to update the index too — it occupies disk, and it evicts genuinely useful data from cache. And they accumulate easily: you add one for a feature, the feature gets removed, the index lives on.

SELECT
    s.schemaname,
    s.relname AS table,
    s.indexrelname AS index,
    s.idx_scan,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s
JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid
WHERE s.idx_scan = 0
  AND NOT i.indisunique
  AND NOT i.indisprimary
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 20;

idx_scan = 0 means the index has never been used for a scan. The query deliberately excludes unique indexes and primary keys — they enforce integrity constraints, so it doesn't matter that no SELECT walks them; you can't drop them.

Two caveats that will save you from shooting your own foot. First, these counts are since the last stats reset or server start — an index for a quarterly report honestly shows zero today and is suddenly essential on the 1st. Look at stats gathered over at least a couple of weeks, ideally a month. Second, index-usage stats are per node: an index idle on the primary may be working hard on a replica you've pointed read-only analytics at. Check every node before you conclude an index is dead. If it all holds up, DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY removes it without locking the table.

What's actually biggest

Sometimes the question is simply "what's eating my disk." pg_total_relation_size answers it, counting a table together with its indexes and TOAST storage:

SELECT
    relname AS table,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC
LIMIT 10;

This diagnoses nothing on its own, but it frames everything else. A bloat ratio matters far more on your three biggest tables, and an "unused" 12 GB index is a very different conversation from an unused 30 MB one. Run it first and you know which rows in every other query above are actually worth caring about.

5. The transaction that hangs and quietly breaks everything

This is the most underrated entry, and it's where section 1's mystery gets solved. An open-but-idle transaction — state idle in transaction — looks harmless. It isn't. It holds whatever locks it took, and worse, it holds back the xmin horizon: autovacuum cannot remove any dead tuple newer than that transaction. So one forgotten transaction — an app grabbed a connection, opened a transaction, and wandered off — adds bloat across the entire database. There's the empty last_autovacuum from section 1, explained: something has been pinning the horizon.

SELECT
    pid,
    state,
    now() - xact_start  AS xact_age,
    now() - query_start AS query_age,
    wait_event_type,
    left(query, 80)     AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state <> 'idle'
  AND xact_start IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start
LIMIT 20;

xact_age is the age of the transaction. An idle in transaction session tens of minutes old is a client that forgot to commit. You can have Postgres deal with these itself: set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout to a minute or two and the server will terminate such sessions automatically.

The companion question is who is blocking whom right now, when something is visibly stuck:

SELECT
    blocked.pid              AS blocked_pid,
    left(blocked.query, 60)  AS blocked_query,
    blocking.pid             AS blocking_pid,
    left(blocking.query, 60) AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking
  ON blocking.pid = ANY (pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid))
WHERE blocked.wait_event_type = 'Lock';

Left side is who's waiting, right side is who they're waiting on. On a busy database this is sometimes a whole chain — one holds, the second waits on the first, the third waits on the second. The pg_blocking_pids() function has been available since PostgreSQL 9.6.

6. The dangerous one: transaction-ID age

Everything so far degrades performance. This one can take the database down, and it's the most silent of the lot. Postgres stamps every row with a 32-bit transaction ID, and those IDs live in a finite, wrapping space. Autovacuum's other job — besides clearing dead rows — is to "freeze" old rows so their IDs can be retired safely. If autovacuum falls far enough behind (often because of exactly the forgotten transaction from section 5), the oldest unfrozen ID keeps aging toward the wraparound limit. Cross it and Postgres stops accepting writes entirely to protect your data — a full outage, from nowhere, on a server whose CPU and disk looked perfectly fine.

You can watch the margin directly:

SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;

xid_age is how many transactions old the oldest unfrozen row in each database is. The hard ceiling is around two billion; autovacuum_freeze_max_age (default 200 million) is where Postgres starts running aggressive anti-wraparound vacuums on its own. If you see a database's age in the high hundreds of millions and climbing, autovacuum isn't keeping up — find what's holding it back (section 5 is the usual culprit) and let it catch up. This is the one number where "we'll deal with it later" can literally mean a hard outage, so it belongs in any check you actually run.

The thirty-second version: one number

If you have no time at all, run just this:

SELECT
    round(blks_hit * 100.0 / nullif(blks_hit + blks_read, 0), 2) AS cache_hit_ratio
FROM pg_stat_database
WHERE datname = current_database();

It's the share of block reads served from cache instead of going to disk. For an OLTP database, healthy is 99% or above. If it's slipped to 90%, either your working set has outgrown shared_buffers, or some query is regularly dragging half a table off disk — which loops you right back to section 3.

The Monday habit

Here's the thing the green dashboards taught everyone the wrong lesson about: most "everything got slow on Friday" incidents were already visible a week earlier in pg_stat_user_tables — a climbing dead-row ratio, a fattening unused index, a transaction quietly aging in pg_stat_activity. Nobody looked, because nothing was on fire yet.

So make it boring and routine. Put these six queries in a file, and run them against your main database once with nothing wrong — just to record a baseline of what "healthy" looks like for you. Then run them every couple of weeks. The whole point is to read the slow rot while it's still a five-minute fix, not a Friday-evening incident: a forgotten transaction you cancel, an unused index you drop, an autovacuum threshold you nudge. Postgres has been telling you all of it the entire time, sitting in the statistics views, waiting for someone to run the SELECT. Be the person who does it on a quiet Monday instead of a bad Friday.

A dead_ratio above ~20% on a large table is worth investigating. And watch for a table where the ratio is high and last_autovacuum is empty — that means autovacuum has never successfully run on it, which is its own red flag (we'll see why in section 5; the whole story converges there).

What to do. If autovacuum is simply behind, lower its threshold for that specific hot table — autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor defaults to 0.2, and dropping it to 0.05 for a busy table makes vacuum trigger far sooner. Bloat that has already accumulated, though, a plain VACUUM won't fix — it marks the space reusable but doesn't hand it back to the OS. VACUUM FULL does, but it takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and rewrites the whole table, so the table is unavailable for the entire operation — not something to run on a live system. The production-safe option is pg_repack, which does almost the same compaction without the long lock, at the cost of disk space for a copy and a requirement that the table have a primary key.

2. What's actually eating the server's time

This is the one query worth enabling an extension for: pg_stat_statements. You very likely already have it — it's on by default in many builds and almost all managed Postgres. If not, add pg_stat_statements to shared_preload_libraries in postgresql.conf, restart, and CREATE EXTENSION pg_stat_statements;. It is the single most useful diagnostic tool Postgres ships.

It accumulates, per unique query, how many times it ran and how much time it consumed:

SELECT
    calls,
    round(total_exec_time::numeric, 1) AS total_ms,
    round(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2)  AS mean_ms,
    round((100 * total_exec_time / sum(total_exec_time) OVER ())::numeric, 1) AS pct,
    query
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 15;

The crucial, slightly counterintuitive part is sorting by total_exec_time, not the average. A query that runs in 4 ms looks harmless — but if it's called two million times a day, it costs your server far more than a three-second report run twice daily. Total time catches that; the mean hides it. The pct column shows what fraction of all database time each query eats, and usually the top three or four rows are your entire performance backlog.

A version note: in PostgreSQL 12 and earlier these columns were total_time and mean_time; 13 split planning from execution, which is where total_exec_time / total_plan_time come from. Queries here are normalized — literal values replaced with $1, $2 — so the same query shape with different parameters collapses into one row, which is exactly what you want. When you've fixed something and want a clean measurement, SELECT pg_stat_statements_reset(); zeroes the stats. Then take the offending query to EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to see where it's actually stalling.

3. Big tables that keep getting read end to end

A sequential scan isn't evil. On a small table the planner deliberately picks one, because reading the whole thing is faster than bouncing through an index. The problem is a large table being read in full, often.

SELECT
    schemaname,
    relname AS table,
    seq_scan,
    idx_scan,
    seq_tup_read,
    CASE WHEN seq_scan > 0 THEN seq_tup_read / seq_scan ELSE 0 END AS avg_rows_per_scan,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(relid)) AS size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE seq_scan > 0
ORDER BY seq_tup_read DESC
LIMIT 20;

seq_tup_read is the total number of rows read by sequential scans. When that number is huge for a big table and idx_scan next to it is small, that table is being walked in full on the regular. avg_rows_per_scan tells you how heavy each individual scan is.

One caveat: this gives you candidates, not a diagnosis. It won't tell you which column to index — only where to look. Pair it with the query from section 2: "here's the statement eating time" plus "here's the table being scanned end to end" usually meet at a single point, and that's where the missing index is. And don't reflexively index a thousand-row table — for that one, the sequential scan is correct. Leave it alone.

Reading the plan EXPLAIN hands you

Sections 2 and 3 both end the same way: you've found a query worth fixing, so you run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on it — and then you're staring at a tree of nodes, which is its own skill. A few things to look for catch most problems without a deep dive:

  • Actual vs estimated rows. Each node shows an estimated rows=... and, with ANALYZE, an actual rows=.... When those differ by orders of magnitude, the planner is working from bad statistics and probably chose a bad plan — often fixed by running ANALYZE on the table or raising its statistics target.
  • The expensive node. Read inner to outer and find where the actual time piles up. That node doing the most work is your target, not the whole tree.
  • Buffers. With BUFFERS on, each node reports shared hit (served from cache) versus shared read (fetched from disk). A node reading thousands of blocks from disk is exactly the I/O you want to remove, usually with an index or by fetching fewer rows.
  • Rows Removed by Filter. A scan that reads a million rows and filters down to ten is begging for an index on the filtered column.

You don't have to parse two hundred lines of plan by hand. Find the node with the surprising row count or the heavy disk reads, fix that one thing, and re-measure.

4. Indexes that only get in the way

Now the opposite problem. A surplus index isn't just "taking up space." It slows down every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE on its table — each write has to update the index too — it occupies disk, and it evicts genuinely useful data from cache. And they accumulate easily: you add one for a feature, the feature gets removed, the index lives on.

SELECT
    s.schemaname,
    s.relname AS table,
    s.indexrelname AS index,
    s.idx_scan,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s
JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid
WHERE s.idx_scan = 0
  AND NOT i.indisunique
  AND NOT i.indisprimary
ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC
LIMIT 20;

idx_scan = 0 means the index has never been used for a scan. The query deliberately excludes unique indexes and primary keys — they enforce integrity constraints, so it doesn't matter that no SELECT walks them; you can't drop them.

Two caveats that will save you from shooting your own foot. First, these counts are since the last stats reset or server start — an index for a quarterly report honestly shows zero today and is suddenly essential on the 1st. Look at stats gathered over at least a couple of weeks, ideally a month. Second, index-usage stats are per node: an index idle on the primary may be working hard on a replica you've pointed read-only analytics at. Check every node before you conclude an index is dead. If it all holds up, DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY removes it without locking the table.

What's actually biggest

Sometimes the question is simply "what's eating my disk." pg_total_relation_size answers it, counting a table together with its indexes and TOAST storage:

SELECT
    relname AS table,
    pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(relid)) AS total_size
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(relid) DESC
LIMIT 10;

This diagnoses nothing on its own, but it frames everything else. A bloat ratio matters far more on your three biggest tables, and an "unused" 12 GB index is a very different conversation from an unused 30 MB one. Run it first and you know which rows in every other query above are actually worth caring about.

5. The transaction that hangs and quietly breaks everything

This is the most underrated entry, and it's where section 1's mystery gets solved. An open-but-idle transaction — state idle in transaction — looks harmless. It isn't. It holds whatever locks it took, and worse, it holds back the xmin horizon: autovacuum cannot remove any dead tuple newer than that transaction. So one forgotten transaction — an app grabbed a connection, opened a transaction, and wandered off — adds bloat across the entire database. There's the empty last_autovacuum from section 1, explained: something has been pinning the horizon.

SELECT
    pid,
    state,
    now() - xact_start  AS xact_age,
    now() - query_start AS query_age,
    wait_event_type,
    left(query, 80)     AS query
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE state <> 'idle'
  AND xact_start IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY xact_start
LIMIT 20;

xact_age is the age of the transaction. An idle in transaction session tens of minutes old is a client that forgot to commit. You can have Postgres deal with these itself: set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout to a minute or two and the server will terminate such sessions automatically.

The companion question is who is blocking whom right now, when something is visibly stuck:

SELECT
    blocked.pid              AS blocked_pid,
    left(blocked.query, 60)  AS blocked_query,
    blocking.pid             AS blocking_pid,
    left(blocking.query, 60) AS blocking_query
FROM pg_stat_activity blocked
JOIN pg_stat_activity blocking
  ON blocking.pid = ANY (pg_blocking_pids(blocked.pid))
WHERE blocked.wait_event_type = 'Lock';

Left side is who's waiting, right side is who they're waiting on. On a busy database this is sometimes a whole chain — one holds, the second waits on the first, the third waits on the second. The pg_blocking_pids() function has been available since PostgreSQL 9.6.

6. The dangerous one: transaction-ID age

Everything so far degrades performance. This one can take the database down, and it's the most silent of the lot. Postgres stamps every row with a 32-bit transaction ID, and those IDs live in a finite, wrapping space. Autovacuum's other job — besides clearing dead rows — is to "freeze" old rows so their IDs can be retired safely. If autovacuum falls far enough behind (often because of exactly the forgotten transaction from section 5), the oldest unfrozen ID keeps aging toward the wraparound limit. Cross it and Postgres stops accepting writes entirely to protect your data — a full outage, from nowhere, on a server whose CPU and disk looked perfectly fine.

You can watch the margin directly:

SELECT datname, age(datfrozenxid) AS xid_age
FROM pg_database
ORDER BY age(datfrozenxid) DESC;

xid_age is how many transactions old the oldest unfrozen row in each database is. The hard ceiling is around two billion; autovacuum_freeze_max_age (default 200 million) is where Postgres starts running aggressive anti-wraparound vacuums on its own. If you see a database's age in the high hundreds of millions and climbing, autovacuum isn't keeping up — find what's holding it back (section 5 is the usual culprit) and let it catch up. This is the one number where "we'll deal with it later" can literally mean a hard outage, so it belongs in any check you actually run.

The thirty-second version: one number

If you have no time at all, run just this:

SELECT
    round(blks_hit * 100.0 / nullif(blks_hit + blks_read, 0), 2) AS cache_hit_ratio
FROM pg_stat_database
WHERE datname = current_database();

It's the share of block reads served from cache instead of going to disk. For an OLTP database, healthy is 99% or above. If it's slipped to 90%, either your working set has outgrown shared_buffers, or some query is regularly dragging half a table off disk — which loops you right back to section 3.

The Monday habit

Here's the thing the green dashboards taught everyone the wrong lesson about: most "everything got slow on Friday" incidents were already visible a week earlier in pg_stat_user_tables — a climbing dead-row ratio, a fattening unused index, a transaction quietly aging in pg_stat_activity. Nobody looked, because nothing was on fire yet.

So make it boring and routine. Put these six queries in a file, and run them against your main database once with nothing wrong — just to record a baseline of what "healthy" looks like for you. Then run them every couple of weeks. The whole point is to read the slow rot while it's still a five-minute fix, not a Friday-evening incident: a forgotten transaction you cancel, an unused index you drop, an autovacuum threshold you nudge. Postgres has been telling you all of it the entire time, sitting in the statistics views, waiting for someone to run the SELECT. Be the person who does it on a quiet Monday instead of a bad Friday.
keyPoints: Your Postgres Is Quietly Rotting — Here Are the Queries That Show It

fullArticle:
It's Friday evening. An endpoint that normally answers in 200 milliseconds is suddenly taking eight seconds. You open Grafana. Every graph is green. CPU is calm, memory is fine, the disk isn't full. By every dashboard you have, the database is healthy.

This is the failure mode monitoring is worst at: the server is unmistakably alive, so nothing alerts, while inside the database something is slowly rotting. A table has bloated. An index nobody uses is dragging down every INSERT. A forgotten transaction is sitting open, holding a lock and quietly making everything worse. None of it crashes. It just degrades, a little at a time, until one Friday evening it tips over.

title: July 10, 1856: The birth of Nicola Tesla
summary: El inventor Nikola Tesla nació el 10 de julio de 1856 en lo que hoy es Croacia (entonces parte del Imperio austríaco). En 1899, Tesla registró ondas de radio cósmicas en su laboratorio de Colorado Springs, Colorado. Aunque creía que las ondas eran señales extraterrestres de otro planeta, hoy sabemos que son...
imageAlt: July 10, 1856: The birth of Nicola Tesla

En 1899, Tesla registró ondas de radio cósmicas en su laboratorio de Colorado Springs, Colorado. Aunque creía que las ondas eran señales extraterrestres de otro planeta, hoy sabemos que son...
whyMatters: Este reporte aborda un desarrollo de alto impacto en el ámbito de ciencia.

multimedia.alt: July 10, 1856: The birth of Nicola Tesla
multimedia.caption: Imagen de portada del artículo

fullText:
Inventor Nikola Tesla was born July 10, 1856, in what is now Croatia (then part of the Austrian Empire). In 1899, Tesla recorded cosmic radio waves in his Colorado Springs, Colorado, laboratory. Though he believed the waves were extraterrestrial signals from another planet, today we know that they are part of the spectrum of an electromagnetic field emitted by astronomical objects, leading to Tesla being known as the “father of radio astronomy.”

In 1900, Tesla began work on Wardeclyffe wireless broadcasting tower on Long Island, New York, planning for it facilitate communication with the other planets in the solar system. Though funding for the project fell apart before it was completed, many of his ideas were foundational to today’s telecommunications industry. His work with remote controls was also a precursor to future space investigation: He demonstrated a radio-guided boat in 1898, laying the groundwork for computer-guided spaceships and robotic cosmic exploration.

Elisa Neckar is senior production editor of Astronomy magazine, and the editor of the Today in the History of Astronomy feature on Astronomy.com.
fullArticle:
El inventor Nikola Tesla nació el 10 de julio de 1856 en lo que hoy es Croacia (entonces parte del Imperio austríaco). En 1899, Tesla registró ondas de radio cósmicas en su laboratorio de Colorado Springs, Colorado. Aunque creía que las ondas eran señales extraterrestres de otro planeta, hoy sabemos que son parte del espectro de un campo electromagnético emitido por objetos astronómicos, lo que llevó a que Tesla fuera conocido como el “padre de la radioastronomía”.

En 1900, Tesla comenzó a trabajar en la torre de transmisión inalámbrica Wardeclyffe en Long Island, Nueva York, con el objetivo de facilitar la comunicación con otros planetas del sistema solar. Aunque la financiación para el proyecto se desmoronó antes de que se completara, muchas de sus ideas fueron fundamentales para la industria de las telecomunicaciones actual. Su trabajo con controles remotos también fue un precursor de la futura investigación espacial: demostró un barco guiado por radio en 1898, sentando las bases para las naves espaciales guiadas por computadora y la exploración cósmica robótica.

Elisa Neckar es editora senior de producción de la revista Astronomy y editora del artículo Today in the History of Astronomy en Astronomy.com.

fullArticle:
Before dawn on July 11, early risers will be treated to a striking celestial scene low in the east-northeast sky.

A slender waning crescent moon, softly glowing with earthshine, will appear to align with the orange light of Mars, the orange-red star Aldebaran, with the sparkling Pleiades and Hyades star clusters close by. Together, they will form a beautiful morning tableau in Taurus — one well worth setting an alarm to see.

Most obvious is, of course, the moon, appearing as a lovely waning crescent, 14% illuminated. It rises around 2:00 a.m. local daylight time and will likely appear as a thin arc of light enclosing a ghostly ball. Here is one of nature's beautiful sights and fits the old saying, "the old moon in the new moon's arms." Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519) was the first to recog...

Tomemos como ejemplo al tío Chang, un amigo de la familia de 67 años que recientemente v...
imageAlt: Older adults know AI is slop. They just like it
aiSummary:
Los jóvenes parecen no poder dejar de ver videos de IA de gatos hablando y frutas engañándose unos a otros. Las personas mayores disfrutan de un tipo diferente de contenido generado por IA, que les brinda la comodidad y el compañerismo que tanto necesitan.

Tomemos como ejemplo al tío Chang, un amigo de la familia de 67 años que recientemente v...
whyMatters: Este reporte aborda un desarrollo de alto impacto en el ámbito de estilo.

multimedia.alt: Older adults know AI is slop. They just like it
multimedia.caption: A smiling baby and an older man are depicted, blending faces in a vibrant digital background with abstract lines and shapes

fullText:
Young people can’t seem to stop watching AI slop videos of cats talking and fruits cheating on each other. Older people are enjoying a different kind of AI-generated content, which provides them with much-needed comfort and companionship.

Take Uncle Chang, a 67-year-old family friend who recently visited New York from Taiwan. As we chatted, the retired businessman showed me some YouTube videos that made him cry. In one, an AI-generated young blonde woman named Rose Bennett performs “Whiskey Was Louder Than Me,” a song about growing up with an alcoholic father after her mother passed away. In another, Rose sings “Brother Became My Father” together with her brother (also AI). Their AI father and the AI audience were in tears.

The videos reminded Chang of his own childhood. His mother, too, had left him, after suffering from his father’s beating. He was eventually raised by his older sisters. “To me, it was ‘sisters became my mother,’” Chang said. “These songs tell such touching stories.”

Chang’s experience reminded me of the “AI family” videos that are getting popular on Chinese social media. On TikTok-like platforms Douyin and Kuaishou, AI-generated chubby babies or handsome adult sons send daily blessings, tell viewers how much they miss them, and bring along virtual roses. Some AI influencers even take on the role of the elderly audience’s virtual lovers.

Tianqi Song, a Ph.D. candidate at the National University of Singapore, and her fellow researchers reviewed more than 200 videos featuring AI family members and interviewed 16 Chinese internet users who watch these videos. Aged between 50 and 75, the viewers grew up in large households but now have much smaller families, partly due to China’s now-abolished one-child policy.

AI-generated chubby babies or handsome adult sons send daily blessings, tell viewers how much they miss them, and bring along virtual roses.

The researchers found that the AI characters offer what real-world family members often do not. They express love directly (rare in Chinese families), show a higher level of filial piety, and talk about health and historical topics relevant to older people. One viewer told researchers that AI content had touched on experiences from her youth that her actual children, who hadn’t lived through the same history, couldn’t relate to. The AI videos also use soundtracks of folk music familiar to the older generation.

The viewers are well aware they are watching AI content, Song told me, and some feel proud they are embracing the latest technology. They also understand that AI can be exploited by scammers. Some operators of AI influencers profit by selling products, and viewers have gladly placed orders. “They know it’s AI,” Song said. “But the joy and companionship it brings is real.”

Many economies are grappling with a fast-aging population and a shortage of caregivers. Seniors need not only food and healthcare but also entertainment and companionship. AI could expand the elderly care options, with products like AI robot dolls and smart speakers already being deployed for seniors in South Korea and the U.S.

Older people are consuming these AI products not because they are more credulous but because they find AI to be genuinely helpful and fun. In a way, it’s similar to young people finding inspiration in pop stars or seeking virtual relationships through otome romance-simulation games. As AI becomes a part of aging life, we should also pay attention to potential risks concerning privacy, addiction, and how AI providers are profiting from older people’s emotional needs.

Uncle Chang suspected his beloved singers were AI-generated after a few plays — the Bennett family’s singing seemed too good to be real. He confirmed this with Gemini, which has become his virtual assistant for everything from navigation to caring for his bee farm. Chang still appreciates the music videos, although he no longer cries over them as much as he used to. “Now I know they are AI-generated,” he said. “They have become a bit less moving.”

What seems like a rescue could prove to be a retreat, as Chery leads its rivals into the heart of Europe’s car industry.

Backed by the state, Chinese workers are using AI to deal with limited resources and drive innovation to compete with Silicon Valley.

Sites from Tencent, ByteDance, and Baidu set curbs like daily word limits for authors and stricter standards to combat poor-quality automated fiction.
fullArticle:
Los jóvenes parecen no poder dejar de ver videos de IA de gatos hablando y frutas engañándose unos a otros. Las personas mayores disfrutan de un tipo diferente de contenido generado por IA, que les brinda la comodidad y el compañerismo que tanto necesitan.

Tomemos como ejemplo al tío Chang, un amigo de la familia de 67 años que recientemente visitó Nueva York desde Taiwán. Mientras charlábamos, el empresario jubilado me mostró algunos videos de YouTube que lo hicieron llorar. En uno, una joven rubia generada por IA llamada Rose Bennett interpreta "Whiskey Was Louder Than Me", una canción sobre crecer con un padre alcohólico después de la muerte de su madre. En otro, Rose canta “Brother Became My Father” junto con su hermano (también AI). Su padre AI y la audiencia AI estaban llorando.

Los videos le recordaron a Chang su propia infancia. Su madre también lo había abandonado, después de sufrir la paliza de su padre. Finalmente fue criado por sus hermanas mayores. "Para mí, fue 'las hermanas se convirtieron en mi madre'", dijo Chang. "Estas canciones cuentan historias tan conmovedoras".

La experiencia de Chang me recordó los videos de la “familia de IA” que se están volviendo populares en las redes sociales chinas. En plataformas similares a TikTok, Douyin y Kuaishou, bebés gorditos o adultos guapos generados por IA...

title: From Kickoff To First Concept: How To Turn Brand Strategy Into Visual Direction
summary: Artículo de Anastasia Sycheva, fundadora de la agencia de branding Feely Studio, que explica la importancia de la fase 'pre-concepto' entre la definición de la estrategia de marca y la creación de la primera dirección visual, para evitar desajustes entre los atributos deseados y el diseño final, con un caso de estudio de una empresa de salud digital.
imageAlt: From Kickoff To First Concept: How To Turn Brand Strategy Into Visual Direction
aiSummary: Artículo de Anastasia Sycheva, fundadora de la agencia de branding Feely Studio, que explica la importancia de la fase 'pre-concepto' entre la definición de la estrategia de marca y la creación de la primera dirección visual, para evitar desajustes entre los atributos deseados y el diseño final, con un caso de estudio de una empresa de salud digital.
keyPoints: La mayoría de los fracasos en proyectos de branding se producen en la fase de estrategia, cuando atributos como 'moderno' o 'confiable' no están definidos con precisión.
La fase 'pre-concepto' es el puente entre la estrategia de marca y la primera dirección visual, donde se alinean percepciones del equipo y se evitan desviaciones.
En el caso de una empresa de salud digital, el atributo 'disruptivo' se reinterpretó para adaptarse a un público de instituciones sanitarias conservadoras, evitando un diseño excesivamente atrevido.
El taller de marca inicial es clave para destapar suposiciones ocultas de los stakeholders y alinear la percepción que se quiere generar en la audiencia.
whyMatters: Este enfoque es estratégico para startups y empresas tecnológicas españolas y europeas, ya que evita pérdidas económicas y de tiempo derivadas de rediseños posteriores por una mala alineación entre la estrategia de marca y su expresión visual. En sectores regulados como el fintech o la salud digital, muy presentes en el ecosistema emprendedor peninsular, garantiza que la transmisión de atributos como 'confiable' o 'disruptivo' se adapte a las expectativas de su público objetivo, sin perder la esencia diferencial de la marca. Además, sienta las bases para una identidad visual coherente a largo plazo, que permita escalar el crecimiento de la empresa en mercados competitivos sin necesidad de redefiniciones constantes.

multimedia.alt: Imagen promocional de los talleres online de diseño y front-end de Smashing Magazine
multimedia.caption: Smashing Magazine ofrece talleres online especializados en diseño y desarrollo web para profesionales del sector

fullText:
Anastasia Sycheva is the founder and art director of Feely Studio, a branding agency for technology startups. Since 2016, she has helped SaaS, fintech, …
More about
Anastasia ↬

Weekly tips on front-end & UX.Trusted by 182,000+ folks.

When a branding project fails, it usually happens long before the logo stage: in the strategy phase, when words like “modern,” “trustworthy,” “premium,” “friendly,” and “disruptive” are left undefined. The result is a gap between what the brand is supposed to communicate and what the designer is expected to create. This is the space I like to call the “pre-concept” phase.

At the beginning of a project, designers usually receive many inputs: a brief, a few stakeholder conversations, competitor references, maybe a moodboard or a list of adjectives. From there, they are expected to create visual concepts that feel right. But “right” is difficult to judge when the team has not agreed on what the brand is supposed to communicate in the first place.

As an example, a health tech company we worked with said they wanted to look modern, trustworthy, and disruptive. At first, “disruptive” sounded like a push toward something bold and unconventional. But as we talked, it became clear that disruption, for them, still had to feel credible inside a conservative healthcare environment. Their clients were large government medical institutions. A brand that felt too rebellious, experimental, or visually loud would not create the right kind of trust.

In other words, their version of “disruptive” looked more traditional than the word suggested.

The problem was not that the client used the wrong language. The problem was that the language was too broad to guide design decisions. Before a designer can turn strategy into a visual concept, those words need to become more specific. What kind of modern? Trustworthy in what way? Disruptive compared to whom? And how far can the brand move away from category expectations before it starts to feel wrong for its audience?

This article is about that pre-concept phase: the work that happens after the kickoff but before the first visual direction. While the broader brand identity process for digital products includes strategy, concepts, implementation, and the assets a product team needs to build consistently, this article focuses on the earlier work that makes the first concept possible: researching the brand context, uncovering hidden assumptions with stakeholders, and turning shared direction into a visual foundation. Rather, a practical bridge between what the brand needs to mean and how it might begin to look.

The first place to build that bridge is the brand workshop, where broad discovery needs to become a clearer understanding of the brand context.

A brand workshop will naturally cover the standard discovery topics: the business, its goals, the product or service, the competitive landscape, and the target audience. This article will not try to list every question a designer should ask in that workshop. For readers who want a broader starting point, we prepared a Brand Workshop Toolkit: Questions and Exercises, a FigJam framework we use in our studio to structure discovery conversations.

Here, I want to focus on a smaller set of questions that are easy to skip but extremely useful before visual work begins. These questions are less about collecting facts and more about clarifying perception. They help the team understand what the brand needs to make people believe, where it needs to feel credible, and which category assumptions it should follow or challenge.

Perception sits at the center of brand discovery because the brand is shaped in someone else’s mind.

If the brand ultimately lives in someone else’s perception, the workshop has to clarify what perception the team is trying to create.

These questions help reveal the assumptions behind people’s opinions, instead of simply adding more opinions to the room. The questions matter because brand attributes often sound aligned before they are actually understood. A stakeholder may say the brand should feel “premium,” and everyone may nod. But one person may mean refined and editorial. Another may mean expensive and exclusive. Another may mean clean, quiet, and minimal. The word sounds shared, but it can lead to three completely different visual systems.

For instance, in the health tech project mentioned earlier, the client described the desired brand as “disruptive.” In many categories, that might suggest something bold, loud, or unconventional. But their audience was large government medical institutions, so disruption had to be expressed through clarity, efficiency, and confidence rather than rebellion. If we had taken the word at face value, the visual direction could easily have moved too far from what their audience would trust.
fullArticle:
Anastasia Sycheva, fundadora y directora de arte de Feely Studio, agencia de branding especializada en startups tecnológicas, lleva desde 2016 ayudando a empresas SaaS y fintech a construir identidades de marca coherentes y efectivas. En su análisis, señala que la mayoría de los fracasos en proyectos de branding no se producen en la fase de diseño del logo, sino mucho antes: en la etapa de estrategia, cuando atributos como 'moderno', 'confiable' o 'disruptivo' se utilizan sin una definición precisa, generando una brecha entre lo que la marca debe comunicar y lo que el equipo de diseño debe crear. A esta etapa intermedia entre el lanzamiento del proyecto y la primera propuesta visual la denomina 'fase pre-concepto'.

[MULTIMEDIA: https://files.smashing.media/partners/smashing/stephanie-walter-free.png]

Para evitar estos desajustes, Sycheva apuesta por estructurar talleres de marca iniciales que vayan más allá de la recopilación de datos básicos como el público objetivo o el panorama competitivo, y se centren en alinear la percepción que el equipo quiere generar en la audiencia. Como ejemplo, cita el caso de una empresa de salud digital con la que trabajó, que definía su marca como 'disruptiva': en un primer momento, este atributo parecía apuntar a un diseño atrevido y poco convencional, pero al profundizar en las conversaciones con los stakeholders, se descubrió que su público objetivo eran grandes instituciones sanitarias gubernamentales, por lo que la 'disrupción' debía expresarse a través de la claridad, la eficiencia y la confianza, no de la rebeldía. Si se hubiera tomado el término al pie de la letra, la dirección visual habría generado desconfianza en su audiencia objetivo.

El objetivo de esta fase pre-concepto es convertir palabras abstractas en bases concretas que guíen las decisiones de diseño, destapando suposiciones ocultas de los miembros del equipo y evitando que atributos que parecen alineados (como 'premium', que para una persona puede significar 'editorial y refinado' y para otra 'exclusivo y caro') generen sistemas visuales completamente diferentes. Para equipos que quieran profundizar en esta metodología, Smashing Magazine, medio especializado en diseño y tecnología, pone a disposición un Kit de Talleres de Marca con preguntas y ejercicios estructurados para facilitar estas conversaciones.

title: Tercer experto estadounidense en ciberseguridad condenado a prisión por colaborar con la banda de ransomware BlackCat/Alphv
summary: Angelo Martino, exnegociador de ransomware, fue sentenciado a 70 meses de prisión por ayudar al grupo ciberdelictivo BlackCat/Alphv, convirtiéndose en el tercer profesional de seguridad de EE.UU. condenado por este delito.
imageAlt: Tercer experto estadounidense en ciberseguridad condenado a prisión por colaborar con la banda de ransomware BlackCat/Alphv
aiSummary: Angelo Martino, exnegociador de ransomware, fue sentenciado a 70 meses de prisión por ayudar al grupo ciberdelictivo BlackCat/Alphv, convirtiéndose en el tercer profesional de seguridad de EE.UU. condenado por este delito.
keyPoints: Angelo Martino, exnegociador de ransomware, recibió una condena de 70 meses de prisión por colaborar con el grupo ciberdelictivo BlackCat/Alphv.
Se trata del tercer experto en ciberseguridad estadounidense condenado por ayudar a bandas de ransomware a cambio de beneficios económicos.
El tribunal federal consideró probado que Martino proporcionó información sensible a la banda para facilitar sus ataques contra organizaciones de todo el mundo.
La sentencia envía un mensaje disuasorio a los profesionales del sector que colaboren con grupos ciberdelictivos, independientemente de su rol.
whyMatters: Esta condena supone un precedente judicial de gran relevancia para el sector de la ciberseguridad a nivel global, especialmente en la Unión Europea, donde los ataques de ransomware han aumentado un 45% en el último año según datos del ENISA. El fallo evidencia que las autoridades persiguen activamente a cualquier profesional que colabore, incluso de forma indirecta, con grupos ciberdelictivos, lo que refuerza la necesidad de estrictos protocolos éticos en empresas de seguridad españolas y europeas. Además, envía un mensaje claro a potenciales colaboradores de bandas como BlackCat/Alphv, que han perpetrado ataques contra hospitales, empresas y administraciones públicas en territorio español en los últimos años, generando pérdidas millonarias.

multimedia.alt: Third US Security Expert Sentenced to Prison for Helping Ransomware Gang
multimedia.caption: Reporte del sector tecnológico

fullArticle:
Un tribunal federal de Estados Unidos ha dictado una nueva condena contra un profesional del sector de la ciberseguridad por colaborar con una banda de ransomware, en un caso que ha generado alarma en la comunidad internacional de seguridad informática. El sentenciado, Angelo Martino, se desempeñaba como negociador de rescates por ransomware hasta su detención en 2023, y fue hallado culpable de proporcionar información sensible y asesoramiento al grupo ciberdelictivo BlackCat, también conocido como Alphv, uno de los grupos más activos y dañinos de los últimos años.

La condena de 70 meses de prisión, superior a la media de sentencias por delitos de este tipo, responde a la gravedad de los perjuicios causados por los ataques del grupo, que han afectado a más de 1.000 organizaciones en todo el mundo, incluyendo varias empresas españolas y entidades públicas de la Unión Europea. Las autoridades estadounidenses destacan que este fallo supone un precedente para disuadir a otros profesionales del sector que puedan plantearse colaborar con grupos ciberdelictivos a cambio de compensaciones económicas, un riesgo que ha aumentado con la proliferación de ataques de ransomware en los últimos años.

El caso forma parte de una operación internacional más amplia contra el grupo BlackCat/Alphv, que logró desarticular gran parte de su estructura en 2023, aunque aún permanecen células activas que siguen perpetrando ataques contra objetivos europeos. Expertos en ciberseguridad españoles advierten que este tipo de condenas son clave para combatir una amenaza que ha generado pérdidas millonarias a empresas y administraciones de todo el continente.

title: Former BioWare Writer Says EA Always Favored Mass Effect Over Dragon Age
summary:
Following the disappointing sales of Dragon Age: The Veilguard, it's looking like BioWare's fantasy IP won't be making a comeback anytime soon. Former Dragon Age lead writer David Gaider believes that the series may be dead, and the game's poor commercial performance--according to EA's expectations--was likely the final nail in its coffin.

"Throughout the entire time I was there, we were always one breath away from the project being shelved," Gaider said to PC Gamer. "The thing that happened is that we kept releasing games, and it would sell much better than they thought it should, and it kept surprising them."

Gaider added that Mass Effect was treated as EA's favorite child, even if they didn't sell as well as early Dragon Age games did. The writer added that Mass Effect was seen as a "slick, modern RPG" that was easy to sell, whereas Dragon Age was perceived to be an "old-fashioned" story-focused RPG, and any success it experienced was deemed to be a "fluke" by EA.

But would Gaider ever consider returning to the franchise? Absolutely, he said, so long as he was handed the keys to the IP and it wasn't produced under EA. "I think that'd be an interesting thing to do. To go back to the basics of what made Dragon Age appeal to so many people in the first place. And go somewhere dark and dangerous, and do things that will make people upset. I think that's what I would want to do with it," Gaider concluded.

In the aftermath of Dragon Age: The Veilguard, EA laid off a lot of BioWare's staff, leaving behind a "core" team to work on the next Mass Effect game. Other related developments include EA announcing last year that it will go private, as part of a $55 billion leveraged buyout funded by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, Jared Kushner's Affinity Partners, and private equity company Silver Lake. Multiple analysts believe that EA might sell off some of its studios so that it can service its $20 billion debt, and BioWare could be a prime candidate.
